os.title: Operating Systems (OS)
os.description: Comprehensive last-minute notes covering core OS functionality, memory management, process scheduling, and synchronization.

topics.name: Operating System (OS)
topics.explanation: Software that manages hardware/software resources and provides common services for applications.

topics.name: Batch OS
topics.explanation: Processes batches of jobs without interaction.

topics.name: Time-Sharing OS
topics.explanation: Allows multiple users to interact simultaneously.

topics.name: Distributed OS
topics.explanation: Manages separate computers to act as a single system.

topics.name: RTOS (Real-Time OS)
topics.explanation: Designed for time-sensitive applications with specific response deadlines.

topics.name: Kernel
topics.explanation: The core of the OS, managing CPU, memory, and devices.

topics.name: Monolithic Kernel
topics.explanation: All OS services run in kernel space (fast but complex).

topics.name: Microkernel
topics.explanation: Only core services in kernel space; others run in user space (more stable/modular).

topics.name: Shell
topics.explanation: User interface for accessing OS services.

topics.name: Socket
topics.explanation: Communication endpoint for networked machines.

topics.name: Program vs. Process
topics.explanation: A program is an executable file on disk; a process is a program in execution (with stack, heap, data, and text sections).

topics.name: Thread
topics.explanation: The smallest unit of CPU execution; lightweight process sharing memory within a process.

topics.name: Process Table & PCB
topics.explanation: Process Table maintains active processes. PCB (Process Control Block) contains state, ID, registers, for a single process.

topics.name: Zombie vs Orphan Process
topics.explanation: Zombie: A finished process with an entry remaining in the table. Orphan: A child whose parent process has finished.

topics.name: Context Switching
topics.explanation: Saving and restoring CPU state to switch between processes.

topics.name: Scheduling Criteria
topics.explanation: Maximize CPU utilization and fair resource allocation.

topics.name: Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive
topics.explanation: Preemptive: OS can interrupt running process. Non-Preemptive: runs until completion or voluntary release.

topics.name: Algorithms
topics.explanation: FCFS (prone to Convoy Effect), Round Robin (time slices), Priority Scheduling (risk of starvation).

topics.name: Aging
topics.explanation: Increasing priority of long-waiting processes to prevent starvation.

topics.name: Race Condition
topics.explanation: Unexpected results when two processes access shared data simultaneously.

topics.name: Critical Section
topics.explanation: Code section where shared resources are accessed.

topics.name: Semaphore & Mutex
topics.explanation: Semaphore: Signal variable (Binary or Counting). Mutex: Mutual exclusion object with ownership.

topics.name: Deadlock
topics.explanation: Conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait. Handling: Prevention, Avoidance (Banker's Algorithm), Detection, Ignorance.

topics.name: MMU
topics.explanation: Memory Management Unit maps logical addresses to physical addresses.

topics.name: Fragmentation
topics.explanation: Internal (block too big) and External (fragmented free spaces). Solved via Compaction or Paging.

topics.name: Paging vs Segmentation
topics.explanation: Paging: Fixed-size memory division. Segmentation: Logical, variable-sized memory division.

topics.name: Virtual Memory
topics.explanation: Makes main memory appear larger. Demand Paging loads pages only when needed.

topics.name: RAID & Spooling
topics.explanation: RAID: Redundant disks for reliability. Spooling: Buffering data for peripherals like printers.

topics.name: Page Replacement
topics.explanation: Algorithms: LRU, FIFO, Optimal, LFU. Belady's Anomaly affects FIFO (more frames = more page faults).

topics.name: Thrashing
topics.explanation: Performance collapse due to excessive swapping and page faults.

dbms.title: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
dbms.description: Comprehensive DBMS Concepts and SQL Queries, complete with ACID properties, Keys, Normalization, and Advanced SQL.

topics.name: What is DBMS?
topics.explanation: Software designed to store, retrieve, and manage data. It prevents data redundancy, ensures data integrity, provides security, and allows concurrent access.

topics.name: DBMS vs File System
topics.explanation: DBMS has lower redundancy (normalized data), high consistency centralization, robust security (Roles/Permissions), and is highly scalable compared to traditional File Systems.

topics.name: Atomicity
topics.explanation: Transactions are 'all or nothing.' If any part fails, the whole transaction is rolled back.

topics.name: Consistency
topics.explanation: Moves the database from one valid state to another, maintaining all rules and constraints.

topics.name: Isolation
topics.explanation: Transactions occur independently without interference from other concurrent transactions.

topics.name: Durability
topics.explanation: Once committed, changes are permanent, even in case of a complete system failure.

topics.name: Super Key
topics.explanation: A set of one or more columns (attributes) that can uniquely identify a row in a table. It is the most general type of key and may contain redundant attributes.

topics.name: Candidate Key
topics.explanation: A minimal Super Key. It contains the exact minimum number of attributes necessary to uniquely identify a row. There are no redundant attributes. A table can have multiple Candidate Keys.

topics.name: Primary Key
topics.explanation: The main Candidate Key selected by the database designer to uniquely identify records. It cannot contain NULL values and must be strictly unique.

topics.name: Foreign Key
topics.explanation: A column or set of columns that references the Primary Key of another table. It establishes a link (relationship) between the data in two tables, ensuring referential integrity.

topics.name: Alternate Key
topics.explanation: Any Candidate Key that was NOT selected to be the Primary Key. They still possess unique identification properties but act as secondary keys.

topics.name: Composite Key
topics.explanation: A Primary Key that consists of two or more columns acting together to uniquely identify a row. Individually, the columns may not be unique.

topics.name: 1NF (First Normal Form)
topics.explanation: Rule 1: Each cell must contain a single (atomic) value. Rule 2: Each record needs to be unique. Cannot contain repeating groups or arrays of data.
topics.example: Instead of [John | Java, C++] in one cell, use two separate rows: [John | Java] and [John | C++].

topics.name: 2NF (Second Normal Form)
topics.explanation: Rule 1: Must already be in 1NF. Rule 2: Ensure no partial dependencies. All non-key attributes must depend on the ENTIRE Primary Key, not just a part of a composite key.

topics.name: 3NF (Third Normal Form)
topics.explanation: Rule 1: Must already be in 2NF. Rule 2: Ensure no transitive dependencies. A non-key attribute cannot depend on another non-key attribute. Everything must depend ONLY on the primary key.

topics.name: BCNF (Boyce-Codd Normal Form)
topics.explanation: A stricter, stronger version of 3NF. Rule: For every non-trivial functional dependency X -> Y, X must be a Super Key.

topics.name: ER Diagrams
topics.explanation: Entity (Real-world object), Attributes (Properties), Relationship (Connection between entities). Cardinality maps like 1:1, 1:N.

topics.name: Concurrency Control
topics.explanation: Isolation issues: Dirty Read, Phantom Read. Resolved via Isolation levels (Read Uncommitted, Serializable, etc).

topics.name: Deadlock
topics.explanation: Two transactions wait for each other. Resolved via Detection or Prevention (Wait-Die, Wound-Wait).

topics.name: Indexing
topics.explanation: Speeds up data retrieval. Includes Primary Index, Clustered, Non-Clustered, and B+/B-Trees.

topics.name: Basic DML
topics.explanation: SELECT (Retrieve), INSERT (Add), UPDATE (Modify), DELETE (Remove).

topics.name: JOINS
topics.explanation: INNER JOIN (Matching in both), LEFT JOIN (All Left + Matching Right), RIGHT JOIN, FULL JOIN (Match in either).
topics.example: SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM Employees e LEFT JOIN Departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

topics.name: Aggregation & Grouping
topics.explanation: Functions like COUNT, SUM, AVG. GROUP BY groups rows, HAVING filters these groups.

topics.name: CTE (Common Table Expression)
topics.explanation: Temporary result set defined within a query's scope.
topics.example: WITH HighEarners AS (SELECT * FROM Emp WHERE salary > 80k) SELECT name FROM HighEarners;

topics.name: Window Functions
topics.explanation: Performs a calculation across a set of table rows related to the current row.

topics.name: CASE Statement
topics.explanation: SQL's equivalent of an IF/ELSE statement.

cn.title: Computer Networks (CN)
cn.description: Comprehensive, highly detailed networking principles covering protocol stacks, transmission mechanics, and critical handshakes.

topics.name: Layer 1: Physical Layer
topics.explanation: Converts logical bits into a physical signal (electrical, optical, radio). Responsible for topology (Star, Mesh, Bus), transmission mode (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex), and bit synchronization. Devices: Hubs, Repeaters, Cables.

topics.name: Layer 2: Data Link Layer (DLL)
topics.explanation:
Ensures node-to-node data transfer. Data is packaged into 'Frames'. 
Key Functions:
• Framing & MAC Addressing (Physical 48-bit address).
• Error Control: Adds CRC or Checksum to detect damaged frames.
• Flow Control: Stop & Wait, Sliding Window protocols.
Devices: Switches, Bridges.

topics.name: Layer 3: Network Layer
topics.explanation:
Handles host-to-host delivery across multiple networks. Data is packaged into 'Packets'.
Key Functions:
• Logical Addressing: Assigns IP addresses (IPv4 / IPv6).
• Routing: Uses algorithms (Distance Vector, Link State) to find the shortest path.
Protocols: IP, ICMP, ARP. Devices: Routers.

topics.name: Layer 4: Transport Layer
topics.explanation:
Process-to-process delivery. Data is packaged into 'Segments'.
Key Functions:
• Segmentation and Reassembly.
• Connection Control: TCP (reliable) vs UDP (unreliable fast).
• Port Addressing: Identifies which application (e.g., Port 80 for HTTP) receives the data.

topics.name: Layer 5: Session Layer
topics.explanation: Establishes, maintains, and terminates connections (sessions) between local and remote applications. Uses checkpoints to resume failed data transfers. Example: Authentication and authorization.

topics.name: Layer 6: Presentation Layer
topics.explanation:
The 'Translator' for the network. Prepares data for the Application Layer.
Key Functions:
• Translation: ASCII to EBCDIC.
• Encryption: SSL/TLS secures the data.
• Compression: Reduces data size.

topics.name: Layer 7: Application Layer
topics.explanation:
Directly interacts with the user. Provides network services to applications.
Protocols: HTTP (Web), FTP (File Transfer), SMTP (Email), DNS (Domain lookup).

topics.name: TCP/IP vs OSI
topics.explanation:
The practical 4-layer model used in the modern Internet.
• Application Layer (Combines OSI L5, L6, L7)
• Transport Layer (OSI L4)
• Internet Layer (OSI L3)
• Network Access Layer (Combines OSI L1, L2).

topics.name: TCP 3-Way Handshake (Connection Setup)
topics.explanation:
Process to establish a reliable TCP connection before sending data.
1) SYN: Client sends SYN (Synchronize) packet to Server.
2) SYN-ACK: Server receives SYN, allocates buffers, sends back SYN-ACK.
3) ACK: Client receives SYN-ACK, replies with ACK (Acknowledge). Connection is now ESTABLISHED.

topics.name: TCP Connection Termination (4-Way Handshake)
topics.explanation:
1) FIN: Client says 'I am done sending'.
2) ACK: Server acknowledges.
3) FIN: Server says 'I am also done'.
4) ACK: Client acknowledges. Connection CLOSED.

topics.name: TCP vs UDP
topics.explanation:
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): Connection-oriented, guarantees delivery, strict ordering, performs error checking & flow control (Sliding Window). Slow.
UDP (User Datagram Protocol): Connectionless, fire-and-forget, no guarantees, fast. Used for VoIP, streaming, gaming.

topics.name: IPv4 Classes
topics.explanation:
32-bit logical address. 
• Class A: 1.0.0.0 to 126.x.x.x (Huge networks)
• Class B: 128.0.0.0 to 191.255.x.x (Medium)
• Class C: 192.0.0.0 to 223.255.255.x (Small, Max 254 hosts)
• Private IP Ranges: 10.x.x.x (A), 172.16.x.x (B), 192.168.x.x (C).

topics.name: MAC Address vs IP Address
topics.explanation: MAC (Media Access Control) is a 48-bit physical address permanently burned into the NIC. IP Address is a 32-bit logical address assigned by the network dynamically.

topics.name: DNS (Domain Name System)
topics.explanation: The 'Phonebook' of the internet. Translates human-readable domain names (www.google.com) into machine IP addresses (142.250.190.46). Uses UDP Port 53.

topics.name: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration)
topics.explanation: Automatically assigns IP addresses to devices. Uses DORA process: Discover (Client), Offer (Server), Request (Client), Acknowledge (Server).

topics.name: ARP & ICMP
topics.explanation:
ARP (Address Resolution Protocol): Finds the MAC Address associated with a known IP Address locally. 
ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol): Used for diagnostics and error reporting. e.g., 'Ping' uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply.

topics.name: What & Why?
topics.explanation: Before sending data, TCP must ensure both client and server are ready, sync sequence numbers, and establish reliability. Without it: data may be lost, duplicate packets confuse the system.

topics.name: Step 1: Client -> SYN
topics.explanation: Client sends SYN=1, seq=x (random number, e.g. 100). Meaning: 'I want to start communication, my starting sequence is 100'. Random seq prevents old packet confusion.

topics.name: Step 2: Server -> SYN + ACK
topics.explanation: Server replies SYN=1, ACK=1, seq=y (e.g. 300), ack=x+1 (101). Meaning: 'Received your request (ack 101), ready. My sequence is 300'. Acknowledges client and starts its own connection.

topics.name: Step 3: Client -> ACK
topics.explanation: Client sends ACK=1, seq=101, ack=y+1 (301). Meaning: 'Received your response, connection established'. Data transfer begins.

topics.name: Interview Q: SYN Flood Attack
topics.explanation: Attacker sends many SYN requests but never completes handshake. Server resources get exhausted waiting for the final ACKs.

topics.name: TCP vs UDP (Analogy)
topics.explanation:
TCP (Reliable): Like sending an important document via courier. Connection established, sent in order, ack sent, lost data is resent. No data loss.
UDP (Unreliable): Like shouting in a crowd. No confirmation, no guarantee, fast. Used for Video streaming / Gaming.

topics.name: What happens when you type google.com?
topics.explanation:
1. DNS Resolution: Checks cache -> local DNS -> root -> TLD -> authoritative server. Returns IP.
2. TCP Connection: 3-way handshake established.
3. HTTP Request: Browser sends GET / HTTP/1.1.
4. Server Response: Sends HTML/CSS/JS.
5. Browser Rendering: Parses code and shows webpage.

topics.name: ARP & Routing Logic
topics.explanation:
ARP: 'Who has this IP? I have -> here is my MAC'. Needed to send data strictly in a local network.
Routing: Data doesn't go direct. Laptop -> Router -> ISP -> Internet -> Server. Routers check IPs and forward like Google Maps.

topics.name: Flow vs Congestion Control
topics.explanation:
Flow Control: Sender is fast, receiver is slow. Solution: Control sending rate (Sliding Window).
Congestion Control: Too much traffic in the network itself. Solution: TCP reduces speed, gradually increases (Like traffic signals).

topics.name: HTTP vs HTTPS & Sockets
topics.explanation:
HTTP is plaintext (hackable). HTTPS is secure/encrypted via SSL/TLS.
Socket Programming: Endpoint communication. Server creates socket & listens. Client connects. Data exchanged (Used in Chat apps).

oop.title: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
oop.description: Complete last-minute OOP notes covering classes, interfaces, inheritance, constructors, and copies.

topics.name: Class
topics.explanation: A logical entity that serves as a blueprint or template for creating objects. It does not occupy memory itself.

topics.name: Object
topics.explanation: A physical entity that is an instance of a class. When an object is created, it occupies memory based on its properties.

topics.name: Static Method
topics.explanation: Belongs to the class. Can be called directly using the class name. Can only access static variables and methods directly.

topics.name: Non-Static Method
topics.explanation: Belongs to an instance (object). Requires an object to be created. Can access both static and non-static data/methods.

topics.name: Abstraction
topics.explanation: Hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential interface.
topics.example: An ATM machine. You withdraw money but don't know the internal cash counting logic.

topics.name: Encapsulation
topics.explanation: Wrapping variables and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access using private modifiers.
topics.example: A medical capsule hiding the medicine inside its shell.

topics.name: Inheritance
topics.explanation: A mechanism where a Child class acquires properties and behaviors from a Parent class to promote code reusability.
topics.example: A Child inheriting physical traits from a Parent.

topics.name: Polymorphism
topics.explanation: Allows a single action to perform differently depending on the context.
topics.example: Compile-Time: Method Overloading. Run-Time: Method Overriding.

topics.name: Method Overloading
topics.explanation: Multiple methods with same name but different parameters in the same class. Compile-time binding.

topics.name: Method Overriding
topics.explanation: Subclass providing its own implementation for a method in the parent class. Requires inheritance, same parameters. Run-time binding.

topics.name: Abstract Class
topics.explanation: When classes share a common identity. Allows both Abstract & Concrete methods. Variables of all types allowed. No multiple inheritance.

topics.name: Interface
topics.explanation: To define a contract (100% Abstraction). Mainly Abstract methods. Allows Multiple Inheritance. Variables are public static final by default.

topics.name: Default Constructor
topics.explanation: Inserted by the compiler if no manual constructor is provided.

topics.name: Parameterized Constructor
topics.explanation: Used to initialize objects with specific values.

topics.name: Copy Constructor
topics.explanation: Creates a new object using the values of an existing object.

topics.name: Private Constructor
topics.explanation: Restricts object creation from outside the class (used in Singleton Pattern).

topics.name: Shallow Copy
topics.explanation: Copies the reference to the original object. Changes in nested objects affect both the original and the copy.

topics.name: Deep Copy
topics.explanation: Recursively copies all objects, creating completely independent instances. Changes in one do not affect the other.
